description: The silky shark is one of the most abundant large sharks in the open ocean, found throughout tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide. Named for its smooth, silky-textured skin, it is a fast-swimming pelagic species often associated with schools of tuna at the surface. Silky sharks are among the most frequently caught sharks in tuna purse-seine fisheries and are heavily impacted by bycatch.
funFact: Silky sharks follow schools of yellowfin and skipjack tuna across the open ocean, and are trapped so frequently in tuna purse-seine nets that they have become one of the most commonly caught sharks in the global industrial tuna fishery.
habitat: Open ocean in tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide. A truly pelagic species found from the surface to depths exceeding 500 m, most commonly near continental shelf edges, oceanic islands, and areas of high productivity associated with tuna aggregations.
diet: Fast-swimming opportunistic predator feeding primarily on schooling tuna (skipjack, yellowfin), mackerel, flying fish, squid, and octopus. Frequently associated with fish-aggregating devices (FADs) and follows tuna schools across the open ocean.
size: Typically 1.8–2.5 m (5.9–8.2 ft); maximum recorded 3.5 m (11.5 ft). Females grow larger than males. Can weigh up to 346 kg (763 lbs) at maximum size.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: No reliable global estimate — historically very abundant; estimated 50–70% decline in heavily fished regions

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Tuna, mackerel, flying fish, squid, octopus

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Moderate — but insufficient to offset current fishing mortality rates in tuna fisheries

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Silky, almost scaleless skin texture reduces drag for high-speed pursuit; highly developed lateral line for detecting tuna school movements across the open ocean

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and warm temperate open ocean worldwide; most abundant in the equatorial Pacific and Indian Ocean

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to 500+ m (1,640+ ft); most commonly 0–50 m near tuna aggregations

threats.name: Tuna Purse-Seine Bycatch
threats.description: The dominant threat. Silky sharks associate with the same surface tuna schools targeted by industrial purse-seine fleets, making them one of the most commonly caught sharks in the global tuna fishery. Millions have been killed over recent decades.

threats.name: Pelagic Longline Bycatch
threats.description: Caught in large numbers on longlines targeting tuna, swordfish, and billfish across the tropics. Longlines represent a secondary but significant source of mortality in addition to purse-seine capture.

threats.name: Shark Fin Trade
threats.description: Silky shark fins are among the most traded shark fins globally. Finning at sea persists in unmonitored high-seas fisheries despite international restrictions.

threats.name: Inadequate Management
threats.description: CITES Appendix II listing (2016) and WCPFC catch limits have had limited effect. IUU fishing is widespread across tropical seas, and observer coverage in purse-seine fisheries remains insufficient.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Tuna
actionItems.description: Select tuna certified by the Marine Stewardship Council or rated Green by Seafood Watch. Pole-and-line and troll-caught tuna have dramatically lower silky shark bycatch than purse-seine or FAD-caught tuna.

actionItems.title: Demand Bycatch Transparency
actionItems.description: Contact tuna brands and retailers to ask about bycatch policies and shark-safe sourcing. Consumer pressure has driven some major tuna brands to adopt FAD-free sourcing commitments.

actionItems.title: Refuse Shark Fin Products
actionItems.description: Avoid purchasing shark fin soup and products. Silky shark fins are among the most common in the fin trade, and consumer demand directly drives finning mortality in high-seas fisheries.

actionItems.title: Support Save Our Seas Foundation
actionItems.description: SOSF funds research into silky shark ecology, pelagic bycatch reduction, and policy work on RFMO shark management across the Indo-Pacific and Atlantic.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Vulnerable — uplisted from Near Threatened in 2014; decline driven by tuna fishery bycatch

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — estimated 50–70% decline in heavily fished areas of the Indian Ocean and Pacific since the 1980s

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — pelagic zones largely intact; warming and deoxygenation are altering the depth distribution of prey

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — tuna and squid populations depleted in key regions by intensive commercial fishing

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — one of the most bycaught sharks on Earth; purse-seine and longline pressure spans the entire tropical ocean

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES Appendix II since 2016; WCPFC limits in place but inconsistently enforced; observer coverage inadequate

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global population count exists. Silky sharks were historically among the most abundant large sharks in tropical waters. Observer data from tuna fisheries documents 50–70% CPUE declines in the Indian Ocean and Pacific since the 1980s. Absolute numbers shown are highly speculative estimates derived from those proportional declines.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Yellowfin and bigeye tuna severely depleted by decades of intensive longline and purse-seine fishing; squid availability limited

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna stocks under heavy pressure from distant-water fleets; skipjack moderately stable but yellowfin declining

preyDeclineRegions.name: Tropical Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Skipjack relatively abundant but yellowfin heavily fished; squid and flying fish depleted in high-effort zones

preyDeclineRegions.name: Caribbean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Moderate prey depletion; some reef fish and squid available but smaller pelagic fish populations reduced

fishingPressureRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense purse-seine and longline activity; IUU fishing widespread; silky shark among the top bycatch species

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indian Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Extensive longline and purse-seine effort; limited observer coverage; fin trade active across the region

fishingPressureRegions.name: Tropical Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Largest tuna purse-seine fishery in the world; silky shark is the dominant shark bycatch species in FAD fishing operations

fishingPressureRegions.name: Caribbean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Moderate longline presence; some national protections; lower pressure than open-ocean tropical zones
